summary: Singspiel opera by Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart (Die Zauberflöte), premiered in 1791. Features the hero Tamino and the comic birdcatcher Papageno on a quest to rescue Pamina from the sorcerer Sarastro, aided by the title enchanted flute and magic bells. Includes the Queen of the Night — one of opera's most demanding coloratura soprano roles — and Masonic symbolism throughout.

works.name: The Magic Flute – The Opera as a Whole
works.description: Comic singspiel opera by Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart, premiered September 30, 1791 at the theater of librettist Emanuel Schikaneder in Vienna. Based loosely on Jean Terrason's pro-Masonic fantasy novel The Life of Sethos. Contains Masonic symbolism throughout. Includes spoken dialogue alternating with musical numbers, characteristic of the singspiel genre. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Composed by Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart; a singspiel opera
examples: name this singspiel by Mozart, titled after a musical instrument
name this Mozart opera which includes the Queen of the Night aria

clues.clue: Premiered at the theater of librettist Emanuel Schikaneder; Schikaneder also debuted the role of Papageno
examples: Name this 1791 opera premiered at the theater of librettist Emanuel Schikaneder.
The role of Papageno was debuted by this man for the premier of The Magic Flute, who was also the opera's librettist.

clues.clue: Josepha Hofer (Aloysia Weber's sister) starred as the Queen of the Night in the premiere
examples: Aloysia's sister Josepha starred as the Queen of the Night in the premiere of this Mozart opera.
Mozart created the villainous Queen of the Night role from this opera for the soprano Josepha Hofer.

clues.clue: Contains Masonic symbols in the libretto; productions frequently use Masonic themes
examples: Productions of this opera frequently use Masonic themes to highlight its supposed underlying symbolism.
A later work in this comedic genre included Masonic symbols in its libretto by Emanuel Schikaneder.

clues.clue: Based on Jean Terrason's pro-Masonic fantasy novel The Life of Sethos
examples: Based on Jean Terrason's nutty pro-Masonic fantasy novel The Life of Sethos, this Mozart opera requires the soprano to cover two octaves...

clues.clue: A dubious theory holds that the composer Joseph Boulogne (Chevalier de Saint-Georges) inspired the villainous Monostatos; another theory holds that Empress Maria Theresa inspired the Queen of the Night
examples: A dubious theory holds that Boulogne was the inspiration for the villainous Monostatos in this Mozart opera. Other theorists claim that Empress Maria Theresa is the basis for this opera's 'Queen of the Night.'

clues.clue: A 2004 Met production inspired by bunraku theatre was staged by Julie Taymor
examples: A 2004 Met production of this opera inspired by bunraku theatre was staged by Julie Taymor.
This director drew on Indonesian shadow puppet theater and bunraku in her acclaimed 2004 Met production of The Magic Flute.

clues.clue: Plot: Tamino is given the title enchanted instrument and sent on a quest to rescue Pamina from Sarastro, accompanied by Papageno
examples: The protagonist of this opera tries to rescue Pamina from Sarastro with the help of the birdcatcher Papageno, on orders from the mysterious Queen of the Night.
Tamino and Pamina undergo several trials with the help of the title instrument.

clues.clue: The title instrument allows its bearers to pass through chambers of fire and water, presided over by the Men in Armour; it can change sorrow into joy and summon wild animals to dance
examples: This object allows its bearers to pass through the chambers of fire and water, presided over by the Men in Armour.
This object has the power to change sorrow into joy, as well as summoning wild animals, who then dance.

clues.clue: Tamino falls in love with Pamina after being shown her portrait by three mysterious ladies
examples: Just before acquiring this object, its owner falls in love with a woman whose picture he is shown by three mysterious ladies.
The opera's protagonist sings 'Dies Bildnis' after getting this woman's picture from the Three Ladies.

clues.clue: In Act I, several characters can only sing 'Hm! Hm! Hm! Hm!' in a quintet after their mouths are padlocked to prevent them from lying
examples: At the end of the first act of this opera, several characters can sing only the sounds 'Hm! Hm! Hm! Hm!' in a quintet, after their mouths have been padlocked to prevent them from lying.
This opera's first scene...includes props like protective magic bells given to a character who stutters 'pa, pa, pa' before having the padlock on his mouth removed.

clues.clue: Act II opens in a palm grove where Sarastro invokes the gods Isis and Osiris
examples: This opera's second act opens in a palm grove in which one character invokes the gods Isis and Osiris.

works.name: "Der Hölle Rache" (Queen of the Night Aria)
works.description: The aria sung by the Queen of the Night in Act II, when Pamina refuses her order to murder Sarastro. One of opera's most technically demanding soprano roles. Requires a high F6 (coloratura soprano). Covers two octaves between F4 and F6. Wikipedia

clues.clue: A soprano must hit a high F6 in this aria
examples: A coloratura soprano is required to sing a high F6 in this opera's aria 'Der Holle Rache kocht in meinem Herzen' depicting the rage of the Queen of the Night.
A soprano in this opera has to hit a high F6 in the aria 'Der Holle Rache.'

clues.clue: Requires the soprano to cover two octaves between F4 and F6
examples: This Mozart opera requires the soprano to cover two octaves between F4 and F6 in 'Der Holle Rache.'

clues.clue: Has a coloratura passage beginning with a turn on A, followed by eight eighth notes on high C, then descending a fifth to F; repeated down a third
examples: One aria in this opera has a coloratura passage beginning with a turn on A, followed by eight eighth notes on high C, and a descent down a fifth to F; that passage is then repeated down a third.

clues.clue: Sung when the Queen of the Night commands Pamina to murder Sarastro; she threatens to disown Pamina if she disobeys
examples: The Queen of the Night sings this technically difficult aria when Pamina refuses her order to murder Sarastro. Reaching F6 several times, it tells of disowning Pamina if she disobeys.

works.name: Papageno – The Birdcatcher
works.description: The comic character of the birdcatcher who accompanies Tamino on his quest. Given magic bells (glockenspiel) by the Queen of the Night. Typically costumed with colorful bird-like attire. The role was debuted by librettist Emanuel Schikaneder himself; Mozart often doubled his vocal lines because Schikaneder was not the best singer. Happily finds a similarly named girlfriend (Papagena) at the end. Wikipedia

clues.clue: A birdcatcher who accompanies Tamino; given magic bells; typically costumed with colorful bird-like clothes
examples: This comical character, typically portrayed with colorful bird-like costumes since he is a birdcatcher, accompanies Tamino on his quest. This character, who was given magic bells, happily finds himself a similarly named girlfriend at the end of The Magic Flute.
This opera's first scene...includes props like protective magic bells given to a character who stutters 'pa, pa, pa' before having the padlock on his mouth removed.

clues.clue: The role was debuted by Emanuel Schikaneder (the librettist); Mozart often doubled his vocal lines in the orchestra because Schikaneder was not the best singer
examples: The role of Papageno was debuted by this man for the premier of The Magic Flute, who was also the opera's librettist. Because he was not the best singer, Mozart often doubled this man's vocal lines in the orchestra.

clues.clue: Uses the magic bells to cause the pursuing Monostatos to happily dance away; also uses them to summon Papagena after being persuaded not to commit suicide
examples: The Queen of the Night gives Papageno this item, which he uses to cause the pursuing Monostatos to happily dance away. He also uses this to summon Papagena after being persuaded not to commit suicide.

works.name: Sarastro – "In diesen heil'gen Hallen"
works.description: Sarastro is the sorcerer/high priest who has captured Pamina — but turns out not to be truly evil. In the aria 'In diesen heil'gen Hallen' (In These Sacred Halls), he forgives the woman who was ordered to stab him. Wikipedia

clues.clue: In the aria 'In diesen heil'gen Hallen,' Sarastro forgives the woman who was ordered to stab him
examples: One character in this opera forgives the woman who was ordered to stab him in the aria 'In diesen heil'gen Hallen.'

clues.clue: Sarastro is the not-actually-evil captor of Pamina
examples: Tamino is given a certain instrument before embarking on a quest to save Pamina from the not-actually-evil Sarastro.

comprehensive_summary:
The Magic Flute (Die Zauberflöte) is a comic singspiel opera by Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart, premiered on September 30, 1791 at the theater of its librettist, Emanuel Schikaneder, in Vienna. It is based loosely on Jean Terrason's pro-Masonic fantasy novel The Life of Sethos, and Masonic symbolism pervades the libretto and stagecraft.

The plot follows the hero Tamino, who is saved from a serpent by three servants of the Queen of the Night in the opening scene. The Queen shows him a portrait of her daughter Pamina and sends him on a quest to rescue her from the sorcerer Sarastro — who turns out not to be evil at all. Tamino is given the enchanted title instrument (which can change sorrow into joy and cause wild animals to dance) along with the comic birdcatcher Papageno, who receives a set of magic bells. Tamino falls in love with Pamina's portrait. Three mysterious spirits (children) guide the pair on their quest. In Act I, a quintet occurs in which several characters can only sing 'Hm! Hm! Hm! Hm!' after their mouths are padlocked. Tamino and Pamina pass through chambers of fire and water guarded by the Men in Armour. Act II opens in a palm grove where Sarastro invokes the gods Isis and Osiris. Papageno uses the magic bells to make the pursuing villain Monostatos dance away, and later to summon Papagena after being talked out of suicide.

The Queen of the Night is one of opera's most demanding coloratura soprano roles. In her Act II aria 'Der Hölle Rache kocht in meinem Herzen' (Hell's Vengeance Boils in My Heart), sung when Pamina refuses her order to murder Sarastro, the soprano must reach F6 and cover two full octaves (F4 to F6). The coloratura passage begins with a turn on A followed by eight eighth notes on high C descending a fifth to F, then repeated down a third. The role was created specifically by Mozart for the soprano Josepha Hofer (the sister of his sister-in-law Aloysia Weber).

Sarastro's bass aria 'In diesen heil'gen Hallen' (In These Sacred Halls) features him forgiving the woman ordered to stab him. The role of Papageno was debuted by Schikaneder himself; because he was not a skilled singer, Mozart often doubled his vocal lines in the orchestra.

Productions have ranged from an 1815 staging in which the Queen descended through a starry sky on a moon, to Julie Taymor's celebrated 2004 Met production inspired by Indonesian shadow puppet theater and Japanese bunraku. Fernando Sor composed an 'Introduction and Variations' on the theme 'Das klinget so herrlich' from the opera. A dubious theory holds that the composer Joseph Boulogne (Chevalier de Saint-Georges) was the inspiration for the villain Monostatos; another holds that Empress Maria Theresa inspired the Queen of the Night.
